attributes.stem: An 81-year-old woman presents with a two day history of left-sided abdominal pain. The pain came on gradually over a few hours and started after a bout of coughing. She has recently been on antibiotics for a chest infection. She is on a variety of medications, but cannot remember their names. Her medical history includes rheumatic mitral stenosis and atrial fibrillation. She has no history of abdominal trauma or surgery. On examination, her blood pressure is 110/74 mmHg, her pulse is 98/min, and her temperature is 37.2oC. Her abdomen is soft apart from an area of tenderness immediately to the left of the midline and maximal in the lower abdomen. An abdominal and pelvic CT scan with oral contrast was performed; a slice of the CT scan at the level of S4/S5 is shown below (Image).
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely explanation for the patient's current problem?
attributes.explanation:
The CT image of this patient shows a large haematoma within her left rectus sheath (Image). Whilst these haematomas are often traumatic in origin, those on anticoagulants are more prone to this problem. Sometimes the trauma may be no more than a bout of severe coughing or straining at stool. Alternatively, the patient may be on an anticoagulant that needs regular monitoring to ensure that the drug is kept with the therapeutic range and the patient does not become over-anticoagulated. That drug is warfarin and administration of other agents can interfere with warfarin metabolism, leading to spontaneous haemorrhage. Antibiotics are the usual culprits, but the risk with ciprofloxacin is very small.

This patient with rheumatic mitral stenosis and atrial fibrillation is most likely to be on anticoagulant therapy. With the advent of the direct-acting oral anticoagulants (DOACs), issues relating to over-anticoagulation and spontaneous bleeding are becoming less of a problem. However, for patients with mitral stenosis requiring anticoagulation (for atrial fibrillation, left atrial thrombus, or an embolic event), vitamin K antagonists (eg, warfarin) are recommended rather than a direct oral anticoagulant (eg, rivaroxaban).

Verapamil or diltiazem are indicated for the initial management of atrial fibrillation to control the ventricular rate. Verapamil may increase the serum concentration of edoxaban (direct acting oral anticoagulant) that increases bleeding risks, however edoxaban is not the preferred anticoagulant in valvular atrial fibrillation.

Diuretic therapy (usually with a loop diuretic such as furosemide) and dietary salt restriction are appropriate when there are manifestations of pulmonary vascular congestion such as exertional shortness of breath, orthopnoea, and/or paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea in patient with valvular atrial fibrillation. Furosemide may cause haemorrhagic bullae but rectus sheath haematoma is not known a complication of furosemide.

Rivaroxaban is known to cause rectus sheath haematoma. However, among patients with rheumatic heart disease-associated atrial fibrillation, vitamin K antagonist therapy (warfarin) leads to a lower rate of cardiovascular events or death than rivaroxaban therapy, without a higher rate of bleeding. Hence, warfarin is the drug of choice and is the agent that this patient is most likely on.
learningPoints: A spontaneous rectus sheath haematoma is usually the result of over-anticoagulation
Warfarin is preferred anticoagulant for atrial fibrillation with rheumatic mitral stenosis.
references: Miranda Allen; Karlin E. Sevensma. Rectus Sheath Hematoma. StatPearls [Internet]. StatPearls Publishing LLC. 2022. Last Update: February 2, 2022. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK519033/

Pankaj Garg, Vipul D. Yagnik, and Gurleen Kaur. Increased Risk of Bleeding with Topical Metronidazole in a Postoperative Wound After Anal Fistula and Hemorrhoid Surgery: A Propensity Score-Matched Case–Control Study. 2022 Feb; 12(1): 133–139.

Mendell J, Zahir H, Matsushima N, et al. Drug-drug interaction studies of cardiovascular drugs involving P-glycoprotein, an efflux transporter, on the pharmacokinetics of edoxaban, an oral factor Xa inhibitor. Am J Cardiovasc Drugs. 2013. 13(5):331-342.
Connolly SJ, Karthikeyan G, Ntsekhe M, et al. Rivaroxaban in Rheumatic Heart Disease-Associated Atrial Fibrillation. N Engl J Med 2022; 387:978.
